title: practical english usage - Part 206
content:
I thought (that) you were in Ireland.

James said (that) he was feeling better.

That can be left out informally after many common reporting verbs.

1  indirect      speech:     He  said     (that)    . . .

We   can often leave out the conjunction that, especially in an informal style.

265 leaving      ou  t  that    _________________ For the relative pronoun that, ►    233.

Given that I'm free on Monday, we could go to Scotland for the weekend.

OK,  I'll help you, seeing that you asked so nicely.

I'll come with you providing that James doesn't mind.

I got here early so that we could have a few minutes alone together.

that, now that.

examples: so that, in order that, provided that, providing that, seeing that, given Some conjunctions are made up of two or more words, including that. Common 9 compound        conjunctions not What do you suppose will happen now? (     What do you suppose that will not Who do you think is outside? (     Who-do-ymHhlnk-fhtifdsunhide?) question word refers to the subject of the subordinate clause.

That is usually dropped in cases like these; it must be dropped when the Noun Clauses,  Direct and  Indirect Speech    Section 24 grammar •  266  interrogative (question-word) clauses Spanish), ►    233;  for relative what (e.g. What she said made me angry), ►    236.

For who, which, etc in relative clauses (e.g. the woman who teaches me It's your business who you invite.  It doesn't matter where we stay.

The  ’ preparatory it' structure is often used with subject clauses (►  268).

You can eat it how you like. (very informal) I'm surprised at how fast she can run.

Where we stay doesn't matter. This is how much  I've done.

Who you invite is your business. A hot bath is what I need.

This structure is often rather informal (especially with how-clauses, ►    492).

(who, what, where, etc) can act as subjects, complements or adverbials.

Besides their use in indirect speech, clauses beginning with question-words 266 interrogative         (que  stion-w    ord)   clauses     ___ Do it the way (that) I showed you.

Look! There are the people (that) we met in Brighton.

relative clause (►   234.4).

We   can usually leave out the relative pronoun that when it is the object in a 5  relative      structures Assuming  (that) nobody gets lost, we'll all meet again here at six o'clock.

You can borrow it provided (that) you bring it back tomorrow.

The garden looks nice now (that) we've got some flowers out.
